- Earthquake loading are considered short term and generally controlled by small strain stiffness. Small strain stiffness is generally 2 to 3 times higher than large strain stiffness. Therefore, for dynamic (seismic) SSI analysis, ks values will be doubled (2 x ks) to account for small strain loading conditions.

![sonic-test](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/gen_227_sonic-test_1.png)

- Thank you for the summary. Just to be clear on terminology, I assume the following from your table below. There are a handful of different types of “pile integrity tests” so I’m avoiding that term to prevent confusion.
- “Sonic Test” = Cross-hole Sonic Logging (CSL)
- “Pile Integrity Test” = Sonic Pulse Echo (SPE)
- Sonic Pulse Echo testing is an inferior test method that is generally less reliable than CSL testing. That is one reason why CSL testing is in the spec. and why it is commonly used for concrete bridge piles. There are a number of factors in our particular case that may contribute to poor or useless results from SPE including large change in soil stiffness, presence and termination of casing near rock, aspect ratio of pile, and stiff rock at toe of pile. If a defect exists, it will be difficult or impossible to detect anything below it with this test. So if a defect exists near the top of the pile, we would actually get less information than if we did CSL testing for a partial length of the pile.
- “Ther
